visualMatch.reason: Both render a clearly humanoid figure from primitives (sphere head, cylinder torso, box arms/legs, box shoes) on a visible green ground plane with a grid and walk-path markers. Aura3D conveys animation via translucent 'ghost' afterimages trailing the walker, though the main figure stands fairly upright with legs together. Three.js shows the main figure mid-stride with legs clearly apart and arms swinging, which reads as a walk-cycle pose more directly. Both are strong prompt matches; the difference is not material.

modifiability.reason: Aura3D output is well factored: a HumanoidRig interface, reusable makeHumanoid/makeArm/makeLeg factories (reused for ghosts), and a single poseRig() parameterized by phase and stride scale. Three.js output is comparably clean with a createLimb() helper, a Limb type, and a centralized applyWalkPose(). Both expose named materials and clear pose math, making edits easy. Roughly equal.

metrics.reason: Three.js achieves the result with fewer lines.
Both null; treated as 0 with no source/notes issues. Note both runs use raw three imports.
Procedural build, no asset paths invented.
Three.js reached a usable render faster.
Both are strong humanoid matches; difference is immaterial.
Both are cleanly factored and easy to modify.

reason: Three.js wins all three non-tied objective metrics (linesOfUserCode, timeToFirstUsableRenderMs, bundleSizeGzipBytes), but the quality dimensions—visual match and modifiability—are tied at 4/4 with no material difference. Aura3D is not strictly better on a majority, so it does not win. The loss condition requires Three.js to be strictly better including visual quality, but visual quality is tied, so the result is a tie per the visual-difference-not-material clause.
